questions.question_text: Under Virginia's Residential Property Disclosure Act, what must the seller of residential property provide to the buyer?
questions.correct_answer: A buyer-beware statement saying the owner makes no representations about the property
distractors: A detailed written list of all known material defects
A one-year warranty covering the property's condition
A certified appraisal of the property's market value
questions.explanation: Virginia uses a buyer-beware approach under the Residential Property Disclosure Act (Va. Code § 55.1-703). The seller furnishes a single standardized disclosure statement saying the owner makes no representations or warranties about the property's condition and that the buyer should exercise due diligence, such as a home inspection, before settlement. It is not a defect-disclosure form like those required in many other states.

questions.question_text: A Virginia seller knows the basement floods but provides the standard buyer-beware disclosure statement. Under Virginia's caveat emptor approach, what is the seller's duty regarding the flooding?
questions.correct_answer: The seller need not volunteer it but cannot conceal it or lie if asked
distractors: The seller must affirmatively disclose it in the statement
The seller has no duty even if the buyer asks directly
The seller must disclose it only after the home inspection
questions.explanation: Virginia follows caveat emptor (Va. Code § 55.1-703). A seller using the buyer-beware disclosure statement is generally not required to volunteer known defects, but the seller may not actively conceal a defect, divert the buyer from discovering it, or lie in response to a direct question. So the seller need not list the flooding but cannot hide it or misrepresent it if asked.

questions.question_text: When must the Virginia residential property disclosure statement be delivered to the buyer?
distractors: Within 10 days of listing the property
questions.explanation: The disclosure statement must be provided to the buyer before or at the time of contract ratification. Late delivery triggers a buyer cancellation right.

questions.question_text: A buyer receives the Virginia property disclosure statement two days after contract ratification. How many days does the buyer have to void the contract?
distractors: No right to void after ratification
questions.explanation: If the disclosure is delivered late (after ratification), the buyer may void the contract within 3 days of receiving the disclosure statement.

questions.question_text: What does the standard Virginia residential property disclosure statement tell buyers about the property's condition?
questions.correct_answer: The owner makes no representations and the buyer should exercise due diligence
distractors: The owner certifies all major systems are functional
The owner warrants the property is free of defects
The owner guarantees clear title to the property
questions.explanation: Virginia's Residential Property Disclosure Act (Va. Code § 55.1-703) uses a single buyer-beware statement. It tells the purchaser that the owner makes no representations or warranties about the condition of the property and that the buyer should exercise whatever due diligence they deem necessary, such as obtaining a home inspection, before settlement.

questions.question_text: The Chesapeake Bay Preservation Act primarily affects properties in which part of Virginia?
distractors: Properties along the James River only
questions.explanation: The Chesapeake Bay Preservation Act applies to localities in eastern Virginia within the Chesapeake Bay watershed. It establishes Resource Protection Areas (RPAs) and Resource Management Areas (RMAs) to protect water quality.

questions.question_text: A buyer is interested in a waterfront lot in eastern Virginia. The agent discovers the lot includes a 100-foot Resource Protection Area. What must be disclosed?
questions.correct_answer: The property is within an RPA zone
distractors: Nothing if the seller chose no representations
Only that a survey is recommended
Only if the buyer plans to build
questions.explanation: Agents must disclose when a property falls within a Resource Protection Area (RPA). RPAs include a 100-foot buffer around tidal and non-tidal wetlands, restricting development to protect the Chesapeake Bay.

questions.question_text: A home near Naval Air Station Oceana in Virginia Beach is listed for sale. What special disclosure may be required?
questions.correct_answer: Military noise and accident potential zones
questions.explanation: Properties near military installations such as those in Virginia Beach and Hampton Roads may be subject to noise zones and accident potential zones. These conditions must be disclosed to prospective buyers.

questions.question_text: What is the width of the vegetated buffer required in a Chesapeake Bay Resource Protection Area?
questions.explanation: RPAs require a 100-foot vegetated buffer around tidal and non-tidal wetlands. This buffer limits land disturbance and helps filter pollutants before they reach the Chesapeake Bay.

questions.question_text: A property is located in a Resource Management Area but NOT in a Resource Protection Area. Which statement is correct?
questions.correct_answer: Development is allowed with best practices
distractors: No development is permitted at all
The same rules as RPAs apply
questions.explanation: Resource Management Areas (RMAs) allow development but require best management practices to minimize environmental impact. They are less restrictive than RPAs, which impose stricter buffer and development limitations.

questions.question_text: A murder occurred in a Virginia home five years ago. Must the listing agent disclose this to prospective buyers?
questions.correct_answer: No, stigmatized events are not material defects
distractors: Yes, all deaths must be disclosed
Only if the buyer asks in writing
questions.explanation: In Virginia, stigmatized property events such as murder, suicide, felony, or an occupant with HIV/AIDS are not considered material defects. Agents have no duty to disclose these events.

questions.question_text: A buyer asks a Virginia agent whether any registered sex offenders live near a listed property. What is the agent's obligation?
questions.correct_answer: Inform the buyer the registry is public
distractors: Search the registry and provide names
Disclose all offenders within one mile
State that no offenders live nearby
questions.explanation: Virginia agents are not required to research or disclose sex offender information. However, they must inform the buyer that the sex offender registry exists and is publicly accessible for the buyer's own research.

questions.question_text: A property uses a septic system instead of public sewer. What must the seller disclose?
questions.correct_answer: The presence of the septic system
distractors: Nothing if the system is functioning
Only if the buyer plans to expand
questions.explanation: Virginia requires disclosure of a septic system. The buyer should be informed so they can arrange for a septic inspection and understand ongoing maintenance obligations.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is considered a stigmatized property condition in Virginia that does NOT require disclosure?
distractors: A failing septic system on the lot
questions.explanation: Under Virginia law, an occupant's HIV/AIDS status is a stigmatized condition and is not a material defect requiring disclosure. Failing septic systems, flooding, and termite damage are physical defects that must be disclosed.

questions.question_text: A seller's home was the site of a drug-related felony arrest. Under Virginia law, is this a material defect?
distractors: Yes, all criminal activity must be disclosed
Only if the felony involved violence
Only within 3 years of the event
questions.explanation: Felonies that occurred on a property are considered stigmatized conditions in Virginia and are not material defects. The agent and seller have no duty to voluntarily disclose this information.

questions.question_text: A buyer purchases a condominium in Virginia and receives the resale certificate. How many days does the buyer have to cancel after receipt?
questions.explanation: After receiving the condominium resale certificate or POA disclosure packet, a Virginia buyer has 3 days to review the documents and cancel the contract if desired.

questions.question_text: A buyer requests the POA disclosure packet for a home in a homeowners association. How long does the POA have to provide it?
questions.explanation: Virginia law gives a Property Owners' Association 14 days from the date of request to deliver the disclosure packet to the buyer. The POA may charge a reasonable fee for preparation.

questions.question_text: Which item would typically be included in a Virginia POA disclosure packet?
questions.correct_answer: Current assessments, fees, and reserve funds
questions.explanation: POA disclosure packets include assessments, fees, pending litigation, reserve fund balances, and community rules and restrictions. These help buyers understand financial obligations and community governance.

questions.question_text: A POA charges $350 to prepare a disclosure packet. Is this permitted under Virginia law?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, a reasonable fee is allowed
distractors: No, disclosure packets must be free
Only if the seller pays the fee
questions.explanation: Virginia law permits the POA to charge a reasonable fee for preparing the disclosure packet. The fee amount is not capped by statute but must be reasonable.

questions.question_text: A condo resale certificate reveals the association is involved in active litigation. How does this affect the buyer?
questions.correct_answer: The buyer can cancel within the review period
distractors: The buyer must accept the risk
questions.explanation: Active litigation disclosed in the resale certificate is important information for the buyer. The buyer may choose to cancel within their 3-day review period if the litigation poses unacceptable risk.

questions.question_text: A Virginia agent cold-calls a prospect who is on the National Do-Not-Call Registry. What has the agent violated?
distractors: No law if calling about real estate
questions.explanation: Both federal and Virginia state Do-Not-Call laws apply to real estate solicitations. Agents must check the registry before making unsolicited calls and comply with both sets of regulations.

questions.question_text: After a listing presentation, what should a Virginia agent leave with the property owner?
distractors: A verbal summary of the agreement
questions.explanation: Virginia licensees must provide copies of all documents to the parties involved. Leaving copies with clients ensures they have records of agreements, disclosures, and other transaction documents.

questions.question_text: A consumer asks a Virginia agent about avoiding telemarketing calls. What should the agent disclose?
distractors: There is no way to stop solicitations
Call blocking is the only option
questions.explanation: Virginia agents should inform consumers about the availability of the National Do-Not-Call Registry. This demonstrates compliance with disclosure requirements and good business practices.

questions.question_text: Agent Martinez receives a signed buyer agency agreement. When must a copy be provided to the buyer?
distractors: Only if the buyer requests one
questions.explanation: Proper document handling requires that copies of all signed documents be provided to the parties at the time of signing. Delaying delivery of copies violates good practice standards.

questions.question_text: Which best describes a Virginia licensee's obligation regarding due diligence in a transaction?
distractors: Only verify what the seller discloses
questions.explanation: Virginia licensees must exercise reasonable care and due diligence throughout the transaction. This includes proper document delivery, timely disclosures, and ensuring all parties receive appropriate information.
